context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Economic Development Theories: Neoclassical and Dependency

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What do the economists say about economic development? Is planning necessary in their models? Are dependency theorists hostile to planning? Are there echoes of dependency theorists in current day criticisms of global governance institutions such as the WTO and the arguments for 'policy space'?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I need help with this reflection on neoclassical vs dependency theories tbh. I’m kinda stuck on how to answer the planning part and the “policy space” connection.
exchanges.tutor: Good, let's start there. Before we dig into the planning question — what do you already know about how neoclassical economists view the role of the state in the economy? Even a rough idea is fine.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is new to the conversation and has identified two specific sticking points: the planning question and the policy space connection. I should start by assessing what they already know before providing any scaffolding. A single opening question about their current understanding will help me gauge where to begin, following the Socratic method.

exchanges.student: My rough take is neoclassical econ usually wants markets to do most of the work, and the state stays kinda limited? But idk if that means no planning at all or just less direct planning.
exchanges.tutor: That's a reasonable starting point. So if markets are trusted to allocate resources efficiently, what does that imply about whether a planner's intervention is *necessary* in that model?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a solid intuition about neoclassical economics. Rather than confirming or expanding on it directly, I should push them to think through the logical implication for planning specifically. This moves the conversation forward toward the assignment's first question.

exchanges.student: I think it implies planning isn’t really necessary in the strong sense, bc prices and competition are supposed to coordinate stuff. Maybe the state only steps in for market failures or basic rules?
exchanges.tutor: Good reasoning. Now flip it around — how do you think dependency theorists view the relationship between developing countries and the global economy? And does that view make them *more* or *less* likely to favor planning?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is reasoning well and has arrived at a nuanced position - planning isn't necessary in the strong sense but there's room for limited intervention. I should affirm the direction without giving the answer, and now push them toward the dependency theory side of the question to keep momentum going.

exchanges.student: I think dependency theorists see poorer countries as kinda trapped in an unequal global system, not just “behind” on development. So that prob makes them more likely to want planning, since the market/global trade setup isn’t neutral in their view.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly — the system itself is seen as rigged, not just slow. Now here's the key connection: if dependency theorists argue developing countries need protection from that unequal system, what do you think 'policy space' means in today's debates about institutions like the WTO?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress and has correctly identified the core dependency theory critique. Now I should push them to connect this to the final part of the prompt - the 'policy space' question and WTO criticism - since that's where they said they were stuck and it's the most complex part of the prompt.

exchanges.student: Maybe “policy space” means countries want room to use their own tools—like tariffs, subsidies, industrial policy—without WTO rules blocking them? That feels kinda like a dependency echo bc it assumes global rules can lock weaker countries into bad positions.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong connection — you've basically identified the through-line of the whole prompt. You now have the building blocks: neoclassical view, dependency view, and the contemporary echo. What do you think the hardest part will be when you sit down to write the 700-800 word reflection?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made an excellent connection between dependency theory and the policy space argument. This is the core insight the prompt is looking for. Rather than dwelling on this point further, I should acknowledge it and redirect them to think about how all these pieces fit together into a coherent reflection piece, since we've now covered the main conceptual ground.

exchanges.student: Prob making it sound like a real reflection and not just 3 separate mini-answers lol. I’m also not fully sure how to phrase whether dependency theorists are “hostile” to planning, bc that seems like a trick question.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct to flag that — why do you think it might be a trick question? What would it mean for dependency theorists to be *hostile* to planning, given what you just said about their view of the global system?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has two concerns: cohesion of the piece, and the nuance of the 'hostile to planning' question. The second one is genuinely substantive and worth exploring briefly through Socratic questioning before addressing the writing concern. I should not help with writing or phrasing, but I can help them think through the conceptual nuance.

exchanges.student: Yeah wait, that wording seems backwards tbh. If they think the system is unequal, they’d usually want stronger state direction, so they’re not hostile to planning—they’re more hostile to laissez-faire or externally imposed rules maybe.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right — you've just answered that part of the prompt yourself. You have solid conceptual footing on all four parts of the question now. The next step is yours: start a draft. I can help you think through any ideas that feel unclear as you write, but the structure and language are on you.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly worked out the nuance of the 'hostile to planning' question on their own. This is good conceptual progress. Since we've now covered all the main conceptual building blocks across about 6 exchanges, and the student's remaining concern is about cohesion in writing, I should remind them that I can't help with the writing itself, and nudge them to start drafting since they clearly have the ideas.

exchanges.student: Got it — I think my main claim is that neoclassical economists downplay planning, while dependency theorists see planning as necessary for escaping unequal global structures. I’ll try drafting around that, but I might come back if my thesis starts sounding too simplistic.
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point. One thing to keep in mind as you draft: both theories are more nuanced than a simple 'planning yes/no' split. Ask yourself if there are any conditions or caveats within each framework worth acknowledging. Good luck — come back when you have something written.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has articulated a clear main claim and is ready to draft. This is good progress. I should briefly note that their thesis is on the right track but hint that there may be more nuance worth capturing - without giving it to them - so they don't produce an oversimplified piece. I should keep it very short and send them off to write.

exchanges.student: Thanks — yeah, I prob need to mention neoclassical people still allow some state role, just not heavy directive planning. I’ll draft it and come back if I get stuck.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly — that nuance will strengthen your reflection. Go draft and come back when you're ready.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is wrapping up and heading to draft. They've shown solid understanding of all the key concepts. A brief, encouraging send-off is appropriate here. No need for more content.

exchanges.student: Will do, thx. If I get stuck making the WTO/policy space link less vague, I’ll ask.
exchanges.tutor: Sounds good — when you're back, bring what you've written and we'll work through any unclear parts together.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is signing off to draft. A brief acknowledgment is all that's needed here.
